THE CALIFORNIA CHANNEL
January 31,1990
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814-0814
To Whom It May Concern:
I am writing to seek the Fair Political Practices Commission's advice on
the need to file a Lobbyist Certification Statement and a Lobbyist Employers
Registration Statement on behalf of the California Channel, a nonprofit tax
exempt educational corporation.
The concept for the California Channel, a proposed statewide public affairs
television channel, evolved from a two year feasibility report conducted at
the USC Annenberg School of Communications. The exhaustive study
demonstrates the lack of state legislative media coverage, studies media
coverage in other states and countries and describes, in detail, the
necessary steps involved in building a public affairs television network
similar to C-SPAN's coverage of Congressional proceedings.
The 325 page academic report recommends that the Legislature install and
operate remote controlled cameras to televise Assembly and Senate floor
proceedings, committee hearings and press conferences. The video signals
would be distributed throughout the Capitol allowing legislators, staff,
lobbyists, the press and visitors to the Capitol to view legislative proceedings
on television sets in offices and designated viewing areas.
The video slenqls would also be available to all media organizations,
including the California Channel which would distribuie programming
around the state to cable television systems, educational inititutions and
public television stations. The California Channel would be funded by
foundation gtants, corporate support and cable television subscribe"-f."..
Recently, we began-the process of meeting with legislators to discuss the
re-port's recommendations and seek their supportTor the concepi"r
tel:vising leg_islative sessions. The senate urrd Ass"-bly have f,otr, rruta
informational hearings on the issue. _Howeygr, there has been no-specific
legislation or resolution introduced. Even if legislation was introduced and
passed, there wol:]d be n9 guarantee that the C-alifo".riu Cfrur"eiio"ta Ue
given access to the Legislaiure's video signal.
10951 W. ptco BLVD, THIRD FLOOR, LOS ANGELES, CA 90064 . (213itS_1015. FAX (213) 474-7083


In a short period, we may meet the contacts test. Do we qualify as a lobbyist and
Iobbyist employer? We believe we may not qualify on two grounds:
(a) No Pending Legislation or Resolution: The California Channel
report is an academic document. While the report urges the
Iegislature to install cameras, there is no pending legislation or
legislative resolution. Our discussions with legislators involve an
explanation of the California Channel report and a discussion on
how a public affairs television network would work.
(b). Media Exemption: The proposed California Channel would be a
noncommercial television network that, as a member of the press
and in its course of business, wants to televise legislative
proceedings. Do members of the electronic media have to register as
Iobbyists when they ask for legislative perrnission to bring in
cameras to televise floor sessions and committ€e hearings? For
example, ifa broadcast organization wanted the Legislature to install
a special conduit that would allow television signals to be transported
from the Capitol to the front lawn, would it have to register as a
lobbyist? In some cases, over 25 contacts with legislators and staff
members would need to be made before the necessary permission (an
administrative action) is granted.
Your advice on this matter would be greatly appreciated. If you should have
any further questions, please do not hesitate to call.
Paul Kop
Executive
,{rt""t
S/-e




Ee
o
to
o-c.9G
tive coverage of the state Capitol. A
collection of educators, business lead-
ers, public interest groups and broad-
casters insist it's an idea long overdue.
And some of the Legislature's key
leaders, upset at the lack of commer-
cial television coverage of the Capitol,
are listening.
"Our polls and focus groups indi-
cate that the public is generally dis-
satisfied with the adequacy of their
current sources of news of state gov-
ernment," says Tracy Westen. an a:-
sistant professbr at the Annenberg
School of Communications, Univer-
sity of Southern California.
Westen was project director and co-
author of a lengthy and well-publicized
study of California's electronic media
coverage of Sacramento. It was less
than flattering. In fact, it was down-
right scathing in its analysis, conclud-
ing: "Californians learn shockingly
little of their state government from
the news media. Those who are sub-
stantially dependent on local television
or radio for daily information live in
virtual ignorance about state political
affairs. "
T) roadcasters themselves are the
-Eliirrt to concede their inadequa-
cies. "I don't think there's any ques-
tion that - 
given all the constraints in
format, time and expense * we've al-
Iowed ourselves to get away from that
kind of coverage," says Vic Biondi,
executive director of the California
Broadcasters Association. "And it's
not just politics. We probably don't
cover as much substantial news in the
community as we should. We could do
better. "
Proposing to fill the void is a non-
profit group called the California
Channel, which is trying to convince
the Legislature that California's law-
makers deserve to be on television,
statewide, perhaps l2 hours a day.
Westen is one of the California Chan-
nel's board members.
The California Channel proposes
that it be allowed to provide viewers
with direct access to the actions of
Gal-$PAllP
A non-prlfit grlup, armed with a neu study, thinks that gr,utng the Capitol
its own tel,wision channel aluW boost democracy, But skeptia say it
ululd harm a priuate satellite struice and draw few uiett)ers.
By STEVE SWATT
f)icture this: It's been a long and tir-
I 
ing day at the office. You havejust
successfully negotiated the Santa
Monica and San Diego freeways dur-
ing the afternoon crunch time. You ar-
rive home about 6:45 p.m., kick off
your shoes. settle into your favorite
easy chair and turn on the television.
With your trusty remote control,
you search for a little audio-visual di-
version. Zap. Dan Rather. Zap. Tom
Brokaw. Zap. Zap. "Hogan's Heroes"
and "He-man ." Zop. The state Assem-
bly, on tape delay, debating the merits
of the governor's proposed budget.
Or how about a45-minute news con-
ference with Gov. George Deukmejian?
Or a Senate Elections and Reappor-
tionment Committee voting to move
California's presidential primary to
mid-March?
Ratings notwithstanding, there's a
move afoot to institute all-out, exhaus-
Steve Swatt is the Capitol corre-
spondent for KCRA (Channel 3) in
Sacramento.
JANUARY 1990 33
i
\
\ ir''
s3
3,
.--


their elected officials. It has modeled
its effort after C-SPAN, which has
been televising congressional proceed-
ings for l0 years and now boasts a
national audience approaching 22
million. Currently, about 5 million
California households are wired for
cable television.
The California Channel would tel-
wise Sacramento political happenings
during a projected four-, eight- or 12-
hour broadcast day. It would offer a
combination of live and taped events,
plus roundtable discussions, even live
audience participation phone-in shows.
Many legislators are intrigued with
the idea of expanded coverage. "Ex-
posure can only be good for this
place," says Assemblyman Richard
Katz (D-Sepulveda). Other legislators
on both sides ofthe aisle seem to agree.
Assemblyman Bruce Bronzan (D-
Fresno) says there could beabuses and
"grandstanding" by some lawmakers,
but "the more light you turn on (the
Legislature), the more people are able
to see government work. " Assembly-
man Tim Leslie (R-Citrus Heights)
calls it a "good idea" that likely will
improve decorum on the floor of the
Assembly.
Heaven knows, the Assembly could
use a dose of decorum, but there's cer-
tainly no guarantee that the presence
of television cameras will do the trick.
Earlier this year, cameras caught the
Assembly's mosl irascible and outspG.
ken member, Dick Floyd (D-Haw-
thorne), going toe-to-toe with Gil
Ferguson ( R-Orange County) during
debate on Floyd's mandatory helmet
bill for motorcyclists.
Ferguson: "Now you're going to tell
us what to do with our heads."
Floyd: "We don't have to tell Mr.
Ferguson that. We know where his
head is - full-time where the sun
doesn't shine."
Then, again, such salty language
from our elected representatives just
might help the ratings.
San Francisco Sen. Quentin KoPP
(Ind.-San Francisco), who has been
known for his oratorical theatrics'
seems exuberant at the thought of
televising the Legislature. "It'll be a
force to compel legislators to be on bet-
ter behavior, to Present themselves
with more grace and consummate
courtesy toward their fellow legisla-
tors.*I think it'll increase the quality of
debate," he says, "and I think ifll have
34 GOLDEN STATE REPORT
a therapeutic effe.t on the Legistaturc. "
Kopp pauses a second, then finishes
off his argument: "And it'll improve
the quatity of dress, too."
Taking the larger view is Bob Con-
nelly, the Assembly's chief administra-
Enltepreneur
Steu Mallory
Adt)ocate
Trac! Westen
tive officer. "The broader the base of
adive participation in the legislative
process, the less there's going to be this
feeling that the Legislature is remote
from the principal interests of the peo-
ple," Connelly says.
Only days after the proposal was
made, the "CaI-SPAN" concept drew
rave reviews from California news-
papers. "It's plainly unhealthy to have
so much important state business con-
ducted out of sight and out of mind," -
intoned the Los Angeles Daily News.
"A TV eye under the Capitol might
hold legislators more accountable. "
the Sqn Frqncisco Examiner editori-
alized, "and it also might provide
triage for the Legislature's bush-
whacked image."
Dut it doesn't take a programming
-E)genius to figure out ihaitelevisin!
the Legislature could be fraught with
pitfalls. For every moment of high
drama, there are hours upon hours of
tedium. Floor sessions rarely start on
time; often they're 45 minutes to an
hour late.
Closed caucuses, called at a mo-
ment's notice, can last for more than
an hour. A bill can be desiSnated a
"special order" for a 10 a.m. debate
and not be taken uP until I l:30 a.m. or
later - or not at all. Thath a lot of
dead airtime, and television demands
tight timetables.
BeYond that, manY of the thousands
of biils are innocuous and devoid of
interest to ihe average Californian'
Will viewers stay tuned to hear an As-
sembly floor debate on AB 564, for
example, which standardizes the
contents of goat milk Producs?
Or will they flip those dials to watch
lawmakers discuss AB 761, which re-
quires the Department of Boating and
Waterways to develop a regional atlas
of shoreline erosion for the Southern
Orange-San Diego counties area?
"Many of the issues are irrelevant
to a vast majority of Californians,"
says Assemblyman John Burton (D-
San Francisco). "I don't know whether
it would make sense or not."
Westen, of course, disagrees. "A
substantial majority (of Californians)
indicate that they would watch some of
the Capitol programs regularly." He
cites a survey taken in late 1987 by the
San Francisco State University Public
Research Institute, in which 89 percent
of the respondents expressed at least
minimal interest in watching Capitol
coverage on a public information
channel.
In its presentation. the California
Channel has the program day mapped
out to the minute: live floor debates
and committee hearings, live press
conferences, talk shows and taped
replays.
Both the Senate and the Assembly
have begun hearings on televising
legislative proceedings, but they're
marching to the beat ofdifferent drum-
mers. "We're exploring the sensibili-
ties of going it alone," according to
one Assembly source, perhaps as early
as mid-1990.
Assembly Speaker Willie Brown has
established a special Assembly task
force to explore TV coverage of the
Legislature and has said he would like
to begin gavel-to-gavel coverage.
Brown believes "the public would ap-
preciate an unfiltered yiew of what
decisions are made by their elected
representatives and how they are
made."
Never mind that many of those de-
cisions are made well out of camera
range, in closed caucuses or informal
settings. During the 1989 legislative
session, leaders arrived at some of
their most imPortant decisions inside
the governor's office, wetl out ofpublic
earshot.
Under the Proposal devised bY the
California Channel - 
one of several
floating around the Capitol-the Leg-
islature would install and oPerate re-
mote-controlled cameras to teleYise
rhe floor debates, committee hearings
and press conferences. The Califomia


t '- -:r ''l- 
- -I
ai 
/
e,
Channel would distribute the coverage
statewide by satellite to cable systems,
public broadcast stations, commercial
news media and educational institu-
tions for use as they see fit.
Some critics, however, question the
wisdom of the Legislature controlling
the cameras. Will lawmakers decide to
restrict radio and teleyision reporters
and technical crews from floor ses-
sions and committee hearings once
gavel-to-gavel feeds are implemented?
Will they turn an altruistic "good gov-
ernment" concept into a self-serving
politicat device?
Bl Allen, co-founder of C-SPAN.
says "there's a certain amount of ma-
nipulation of the media. The main
thing, though, is rhat it's truly un-
edited, gavel-to-gavel coverage. "
Realistically, however, true gavel-
to-gavel coverage of the Legislature is
impossible. On any given day, for ex-
ample, a half-dozen committees might
meet simultaneously. Judgments would
have to be made as to which committee
hearing should be televised. Legisla-
tors given program mir.g carte blanche
can influence public opinion on a vol-
atile issue or help a colleague who
needs a little added exposure during an
election year.
Technically and financially, wiring
the state Capitol for pictures and sound
VouA @ndd N auctage oJ lbe Captlol benefil ot borc ieuers?
is a snap. Currently, legislators, lob-
byists and news organizations receive
live audio feeds of Capitol hearings
and news conferences. Assembly chief
Connelly believes the existing wlring
for the computer systems could be used
to support the cable signal.
"lf you want to generate a signal
from the Ass€mbly and Senate cham-
bers, then you'd have to make some
modifications, but it's minimal," Con-
nelly says. "Generating a signal from
the committee roorns would be a wiring
problem, but itt not insurmountable. "
Connelly says the Assembly, at
least, is likely to look at an internal
system first, allowing each Assembly
member to pick up feeds of ongoing
committee hearings.
Westen estimates thar the cost of in-
stalling the equipment in the state Cap-
itol would range from $850,m0 to $2
million, with annual operating costs of
less than Sl million-
Jnterestingly, the California Channel
lmedia study and proposal come at a
time when many California rclevision
stations app€ar to be increasing, not
decreasing, their coverage of state and
governmental affairs.
True, no out-of-town television
station maintains a Capitol bureau
anymore, but a former NBC news
corrcspondent has stepped in to fill the
void. Steve Mallory created Northern
California News Satellite in 1987. The
service feeds daily steries and inler-
views from Sacramento to l5 tele-
vision stations. From San Diego to
Medford. Ore. - including stations in
the huge Los Angeles market - view-
ers are getting at least a taste of what
their elected representatives are up to.
Mallory, whose investors have sunk
nearly Sl million into the satellite/
news venture, says expanded coverage
of the Capitol is "a terrific, healthy
idea, something that Californians need
without a doubt. "
But he's concerned, quite naturally,
that if a non-profit organization re-
ceives the blessing of the Legislature
for an exclusive audio-video franchise.
he'll be out of business. "l'm opposed
to taxpayers' dollars supporting this,"
he says. "l do it for profit. What they're
proposing is what I do.
"Along come these non-profiteers
{looking) for tax breaks," he contin-
ues. "They are educators, teachers,
public relations people, corporations
and special interest groups (with) no
experience in journalism. "
Mallory also takes exception to
Westen's contention that television has
abandoned the Capitol and ignored
state politics. "The Capitol is being
JANUARY 1990 35
!'
II
--f '-'?'o ilE;,l
- 
t..
. r':+- r:.r.; :-;
' .-.- 
J- :!


covered more than ever," he says.
Biondi, representing the broad-
casters, echoes those words. "I object
to Tracy Westen and his people polish-
ing their own apple by dumping on us.
We're willing to take our lumps. But
there's no connection between that or-
ganization's capability to put that chan-
nsl together and what we don't do."
Contrary to the California Chan-
nel's ambitious plan of government
programming, Biondi proposes that
audio and video sources simply be fed
outside the Capitol building - 
un-
edited and in their entirety - to
television stations, cable facilities, col-
leges and universities. A C-SPAN type
of operation would be merely one of
several possible uses of this enhanced
coverage of the Capitol, Biondi says.
He suggesrc that neither the Legis-
lature nor the governor have any in-
volvement, financially or editorially,
in the condition of the feed or its uses.
The California Channel is perfectly
willing to have the Legislature pick up
part of the tab for start-up and annual
operating costs. It says it would adopt
guidelines to ensure that its coverage
is impartial and balanced.
But a Senate source, who asked not
to be identified, suggests it would be
difficult to prevent lawmakers from
meddling in such programs, "If we're
paying for it. shouldn't we have a voice
in it? After all, a controlled TV helps
them come rc-election time."
Should taxpayers pay for this in the
first place? "I would be concerned
about getting taxpayer money to front
this," Assemblyman Katz says. "If
they can come up with foundation
money, then it makes sense to do."
tTlhere's no doubt that television is
I an accepted - 
and basically un-
obtrusive - 
participant on the floors
of the Legislaturc. Younger legislators
who grew up in the television age tend
to understand the medium, its timita-
tions and prospecls for political
manipulation.
Older lawmakers, however, may be
enchanted with television but fear what
it could show.
Senate Republican Caucus Chair-
man John Doolittle, who has used tele-
vision effectively to enhance his
career, noneth€less takes a jaundiced
view of televised floor sessions. Not-
ing that Senate sessions tend to be de-
liberative and sedate, he suggests that
the presence of cameras will cause
members to "posture and pander.'l
In the Assembly, where debates tend
to be raucous. at rimes profane and in-
terminably long, with or without the
cameras, Doolittle thinks gavel-to-
gavel coverage might force some mem-
bers to clean up their act. "It'U affect
each house differently," the senator
says. "Ours will get wors€. Theirs will
get better. "
But no less an expert than veteran
political consultant Richie Ross con-
tends televising the Legislature will
have no impact whatsoeyer. Ross has
managed several legislative campaigns
and currently is masterminding Atty.
Gen. John Van de Kamp's run for gov-
ernor. He scoffs at surveys indicating
a public appetite for Capitol news.
"This (Sacramento) is the seat of
state government, with more state em-
ployees and all the Capitol employ-
ees," Ross says. "But peoPle who
work in the Capitol never watched
'Sacramento Week in Review. "'
And, he insists, they're not going to
watch televised proceedings from the
state Capitol. "It's boring and irrele-
vant. I don't think anyone is going to
watch it. I wouldn't watch it."

sections.question: Do we qualify as a lobbyist and lobbyist employer? We believe we may not qualify on two grounds: (a) No Pending Legislation or Resolution: The California Channel report is an academic document. While the report urges the legislature to install cameras, there is no pending legislation or legislative resolution. Our discussions with legislators involve an explanation of the California Channel report and a discussion on how a public affairs television network would work. (b) Media Exemption: The proposed California Channel would be a noncommercial television network that, as a member of the press and in its course of business, wants to televise legislative proceedings. Do members of the electronic media have to register as lobbyists when they ask for legislative permission to bring in cameras to televise floor sessions and committee hearings?
sections.question_synthetic: Must the California Channel register as a lobbyist and lobbyist employer when meeting with legislators to discuss televising legislative proceedings, or does a media exemption apply to nonprofit educational television networks seeking access to film legislative sessions?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. Document includes substantial background material (magazine article) that provides context but is not part of the formal advice letter request. No formal CONCLUSION section exists in this document. The extraction confidence is moderate due to the inclusion of extraneous material and informal presentation.

embedding.summary: The California Channel, a nonprofit tax-exempt educational corporation, seeks FPPC advice on whether it must register as a lobbyist and lobbyist employer when meeting with legislators to discuss a proposal to televise legislative proceedings. The organization argues it may not qualify based on the lack of pending legislation and potential media exemption status.
